title: How to Calculate True MRR With Mixed Annual and Monthly Plans
meta_description: Mixing annual and monthly plans makes MRR math messy. Here's how to calculate true MRR without lying to yourself or your investors.
h1: Calculating True MRR When You've Got Annual and Monthly Plans in the Same Stripe Account
body_markdown:
A founder I know closed a $2,400 annual deal on March 3rd and his Stripe dashboard showed his MRR jumped by $2,400 that day. He texted me a screenshot with three fire emojis. I had to be the guy who said hey, that's not how this works — your MRR went up by $200, not $2,400, and also Stripe's showing you gross volume, not MRR at all.

This is the single most common mistake I see in the $1k-$50k MRR range. And it's not because founders are dumb. It's because the moment you mix annual and monthly subscriptions in one Stripe account, the math stops being obvious, and every tool shows you a slightly different number.

So let's actually talk through it. Coffee's already cold, but whatever.

## The one formula that matters

True MRR for a mixed plan book is this:

**Sum of (monthly plan revenue) + Sum of (annual plan revenue ÷ 12)**

That's it. That's the whole thing. If someone pays you $1,200/year, that contributes $100 to MRR. If someone pays you $99/month, that contributes $99. Add them all up across active subscriptions and you have your number.

The reason this gets hard is not the formula. It's everything around it.

What counts as "active"? What do you do with the subscription that's in a 14-day past-due state? What about the annual plan that was paid in January but the customer cancelled in March — do they still count until December, or do they stop counting the day they cancel? What about discounts, credits, pauses, and the customer who downgraded mid-cycle?

I got this wrong for six months when I was running my first product. I was counting cancelled-but-not-yet-expired annual subs in my MRR because hey, the money was in the bank. Technically defensible. Also technically a great way to lie to yourself about growth.

### The "paid isn't the same as recognized" trap

Here's the thing that trips people up. Stripe, by default, shows you charges. A charge happens when money moves. If I pay you $1,200 in January for a year of service, Stripe sees a $1,200 charge in January and zero charges February through December.

But your MRR in February is still $100 from that customer. And March. And every month through the following January. Because they're still an active subscriber — you just already collected the cash.

This is why the Stripe dashboard's "MRR" number (if you've even bothered to find it, buried in the Billing analytics) has always felt weird. Stripe has gotten better at this since their 2023 revenue recognition updates, but if you have any non-trivial mix of plans, don't trust the default views. They're showing you something, but it's not quite MRR in the way you think about it when you're reporting to an investor or your co-founder.

## Where most founders go wrong

I asked around in a Slack group of ~40 bootstrappers last month how they calculate MRR. The answers split roughly like this:

- 14 were using ChartMogul or Baremetrics and trusting whatever number came out
- 11 had a Google Sheet they updated manually (god bless)
- 8 were looking at Stripe's built-in MRR chart and calling it a day
- 5 had some custom script hitting the Stripe API
- 2 admitted they don't really know their MRR and just look at monthly cash in

The Sheet people, honestly? They usually have the most accurate number. Because they had to think about it. The tool-trusters are the ones who get bitten.

Here's a specific failure mode. Baremetrics — which I actually like, to be clear, and their Startup plan at $129/mo is fine for what it is — counts an annual subscription at 1/12th of the paid amount per month. Good. But if you have a customer on a custom annual plan where they paid $10,000 upfront with a 6-month bonus tacked on (so 18 months of service), Baremetrics doesn't always handle the amortization the way you'd expect unless you set up the plan exactly right in Stripe. And most founders don't set it up exactly right in Stripe, because Stripe's subscription schedules are... let's be charitable and call them "a learning curve."

ChartMogul handles this a bit better in my experience. But ChartMogul's pricing starts at a point where most sub-$10k MRR founders balk.

And if you're trying to roll your own off the Stripe API? The `subscription.items.price.unit_amount` field is what you want, combined with `recurring.interval` and `recurring.interval_count`. But you also need to handle `status` filtering (active, trialing, past_due — include or exclude?), plus any discounts on the subscription object, plus proration adjustments. It's maybe 200 lines of Python done carefully. I've written it three times. Each time I thought I had it right. Each time I found an edge case two weeks later.

## The five things you actually have to decide

Before you can calculate "true" MRR, you have to commit to answers for these:

1. **Do trialing subscriptions count?** I say no. A trial isn't revenue until it converts. Stripe by default counts them. I think that's wrong and it inflates your number.
2. **Do past_due subscriptions count?** For the first 7 days, yes. After that, no. Most of those won't recover.
3. **How do you handle discounts?** A $100/mo plan with a 50% forever discount is $50 MRR, not $100. Seems obvious. I've seen founders miss this.
4. **How do you handle one-time charges on a subscription?** They don't count. Setup fees, overage charges, add-ons that aren't recurring — none of it goes in MRR.
5. **What about annual plans paid in installments?** If someone's paying $100/mo on a 12-month commitment, that's $100 MRR. The commitment doesn't change the monthly math.

Pick your answers. Write them down somewhere. Stick to them. The exact choices matter less than consistency — if you change definitions every quarter, your growth chart is fiction.

### The annual plan distortion problem

Here's the part nobody warns you about. When you start selling annual plans, your MRR growth chart gets smoother and your cash flow gets lumpier. Those are both good things on their own but they decouple in a way that can mess with your head.

You'll have months where you collect $30k in cash but your MRR went up by $2k. That's not a problem. That's annual pricing working as intended. But if you're used to eyeballing your bank account as a proxy for business health, you'll either feel like a genius (the cash-heavy months) or a failure (the quiet cash months after a big annual push).

Jason Cohen wrote about this years ago in one of his WP Engine posts — the emotional whiplash of moving to annual billing. He was right then and he's right now. The fix is to stop looking at cash as your growth metric and start looking at MRR, which is exactly the thing that's hard to calculate when you've mixed the two.

Circular? A little.

## What I actually do now

After burning myself on this enough times, here's my current approach. It's not elegant.

I pull the list of all active subscriptions from Stripe once a day. For each one, I calculate its normalized monthly value — monthly plans contribute their price, annual plans contribute price/12, quarterly contributes price/3, and so on. I exclude trialing and anything past_due for more than 7 days. I apply any active coupon or discount. I sum it up.

That number, compared to yesterday's version of that number, is my real growth. Not charges. Not cash collected. Not Stripe's dashboard number.

I check it once a week. Not once an hour. Refreshing MRR is the founder equivalent of checking your ex's Instagram — nothing good happens, and you should be shipping.

(This is the part where I mention that I eventually got tired of running my own script and built [Stripe Revenue Tracker](/) to do exactly this calculation and pipe it somewhere I'd actually see it. If you want the logic but not the cron job, it exists. If you want to keep your Sheet, also fine — the Sheet people are often right.)

The real answer is that "true MRR" is less about a formula and more about a commitment to one definition. Pick yours. A founder doing $8k MRR with a clear definition beats a founder doing "somewhere between $11k and $14k depending on which tool I check" every single time — because the first one knows if they're growing and the second one is guessing.

And guessing, six months in, compounds into not knowing what your business actually is.
target_keyword: calculate true MRR annual and monthly plans
cover_image_prompt: Abstract editorial illustration, dark mode, deep navy and charcoal background. Two intersecting geometric waveforms — one smooth sine wave representing annual revenue, one jagged step function representing monthly revenue — merging into a single glowing line. Subtle grid of faint dots beneath, like a financial chart stripped to its skeleton. Accent color: muted teal and a single warm amber highlight where the waves converge. Minimal, architectural, premium tech magazine aesthetic. Flat vector with subtle gradient depth. No text, no people, no logos, no literal dollar signs.
